<!-- Generated by SpecStory -->

# Previewing GitHub Pages site locally with Jekyll (2025-06-14 16:30:40)

_**User**_

I'm trying to create a Github Pages site with this repository, to use as a personal project portfolio. I'm also trying to use a Jekyll theme. How can I preview the changes I'm making to my site without constantly pushing repo commits and looking at the live site?

---

_**Assistant**_

You can preview your GitHub Pages site locally using Jekyll before pushing commits. Here’s how:

1. **Install Ruby & Bundler**  
   - Download and install [Ruby+Devkit for Windows](https://rubyinstaller.org/).
   - Open a new terminal and run:
     ```
     gem install bundler
     ```

2. **Install Jekyll & Dependencies**  
   - In your project folder, run:
     ```
     bundle init
     ```
   - Add Jekyll and your theme to the `Gemfile`:
     ```
     bundle add jekyll
     bundle add jekyll-theme-YOUR-THEME-NAME
     ```
   - Install dependencies:
     ```
     bundle install
     ```

3. **Serve Your Site Locally**  
   - In your project folder, run:
     ```
     bundle exec jekyll serve
     ```
   - Open [http://localhost:4000](http://localhost:4000) in your browser to preview your site.

**Tip:**  
You can edit files and refresh the browser to see changes instantly—no need to push to GitHub until you’re ready.

Let me know if you need help with a specific Jekyll theme or run into errors!   - Open [http://localhost:4000](http://localhost:4000) in your browser to preview your site.

**Tip:**  
You can edit files and refresh the browser to see changes instantly—no need to push to GitHub until you’re ready.

Let me know if you need help with a specific Jekyll theme or run into errors!

---

_**User**_

Can I include several themes to try them out, and just select one of them at a time?

---

_**Assistant**_

Yes, you can include several Jekyll themes in your `Gemfile` and switch between them by changing the `theme:` setting in your `_config.yml` file.

**Steps:**

1. **Add Multiple Themes to Gemfile**  
   For example:
   ````ruby
   # filepath: Gemfile
   # ...existing code...
   gem "jekyll-theme-cayman"
   gem "jekyll-theme-minimal"
   gem "jekyll-theme-midnight"
   # ...existing code...
   ````

2. **Install the Themes**  
   ```
   bundle install
   ```

3. **Switch Themes in `_config.yml`**  
   Change the `theme:` line to the theme you want to use:
   ````yaml
   # filepath: _config.yml
   # ...existing code...
   theme: jekyll-theme-cayman
   # ...existing code...
   ````

   To try another, just change the value (e.g., `jekyll-theme-minimal`) and restart the server.

4. **Restart Jekyll Server**  
   After changing the theme, stop and restart your local server:
   ```
   bundle exec jekyll serve
   ```

**Note:**  
Only one theme can be active at a time. You can quickly switch by editing `_config.yml`.

---

_**User**_

What themes do you think would be good for a portfolio / blog / personal website?

---

_**Assistant**_

Here are some popular Jekyll themes that work well for portfolios, blogs, and personal websites:

### Minimal & Clean
- **jekyll-theme-minimal** – Simple, clean, and easy to customize.
- **jekyll-theme-cayman** – Modern look with accent colors.
- **jekyll-theme-leap-day** – Friendly and approachable.

### Portfolio-Focused
- **jekyll-theme-primer** – GitHub’s own theme, clean and professional.
- **Minimal Mistakes** ([link](https://mmistakes.github.io/minimal-mistakes/)) – Highly customizable, great for portfolios and blogs.
- **Chirpy** ([link](https://github.com/cotes2020/jekyll-theme-chirpy)) – Modern, feature-rich, and blog-friendly.

### Blog-Oriented
- **jekyll-theme-midnight** – Dark, stylish, and good for blogs.
- **Tale** ([link](https://github.com/chesterhow/tale)) – Simple and elegant for personal blogs.

### Personal Website
- **So Simple** ([link](https://github.com/mmistakes/so-simple-theme)) – Clean, with support for portfolios and blogs.
- **Lanyon** ([link](https://github.com/poole/lanyon)) – Minimal sidebar layout, good for personal sites.

**Tip:**  
You can browse more themes at [Jekyll Themes](https://jekyllthemes.io/) or [GitHub Pages Themes](https://pages.github.com/themes/).

Let me know if you want help installing or previewing any of these!

---

_**User**_

Does GitHub Pages only support the themes at this link?
https://pages.github.com/themes/

---

_**Assistant**_

No, GitHub Pages supports more than just the themes listed at [https://pages.github.com/themes/](https://pages.github.com/themes/).

- **Official themes:** The ones at that link are the “official” supported themes, and you can use them easily by setting the `theme:` key in your `_config.yml`.
- **Other themes:** You can use almost any Jekyll theme (including custom or third-party themes) by adding them to your repository and configuring your site to use them as a “remote theme” or as a local theme.

**How to use other themes:**
- For remote themes, add this to your `_config.yml`:
  ````yaml
  remote_theme: owner/repo
  ````
  (Replace `owner/repo` with the GitHub repo of the theme.)

- For custom/local themes, copy the theme files into your repo and configure as needed.

**Note:**  
If you use a custom or remote theme, you must build your site locally and push the generated `_site` folder if you’re using GitHub Pages’ “static files” mode, or enable GitHub Pages’ “GitHub Actions” or “Build from source” options.

Let me know if you want help setting up a specific theme!

---

_**User**_

The Minimal Mistakes theme looks really nice, does it have the equivalent of a dark mode?

---

_**Assistant**_

Yes, **Minimal Mistakes** supports dark mode!

- It has a built-in “dark” skin, as well as several other color schemes.
- You can set the default skin in your `_config.yml`:
  ````yaml
  # filepath: _config.yml
  # ...existing code...
  minimal_mistakes_skin: "dark"
  # ...existing code...
  ````
- Users can also toggle between light and dark modes using the site’s UI (if you enable the skin switcher).

**Documentation:**  
See the [Minimal Mistakes Skins documentation](https://mmistakes.github.io/minimal-mistakes/docs/skins/) for details and screenshots.

Let me know if you want help setting up Minimal Mistakes or enabling the skin switcher!

---

_**User**_

So what if I want to be able to switch between a supported theme (i.e. using the theme: line in _config.yml) and a remote theme (e.g. using the remote_theme: line)

---

_**Assistant**_

You can only use **one** of `theme:` or `remote_theme:` in your `_config.yml` at a time—Jekyll and GitHub Pages do not support both simultaneously. To switch between them:

1. **For a supported theme:**  
   Use only the `theme:` line:
   ````yaml
   # filepath: _config.yml
   theme: jekyll-theme-cayman
   # remote_theme: owner/repo  # Commented out or removed
   ````

2. **For a remote theme:**  
   Use only the `remote_theme:` line:
   ````yaml
   # filepath: _config.yml
   # theme: jekyll-theme-cayman  # Commented out or removed
   remote_theme: mmistakes/minimal-mistakes
   ````

**To switch:**  
- Comment out or remove the line you’re not using.
- Save the file and restart your Jekyll server if running locally.

**Tip:**  
You can keep both lines in your `_config.yml` and just comment/uncomment the one you want to use for easy switching.

Let me know if you want an example or a script to automate this!

_**User**_

How would I use the chirpy theme?

---

_**Assistant**_

To use the **Chirpy** Jekyll theme on GitHub Pages, follow these steps:

---

### 1. **Fork the Chirpy Starter Repo**

Go to [https://github.com/cotes2020/chirpy-starter](https://github.com/cotes2020/chirpy-starter) and click **Fork** to create your own copy.

---

### 2. **Clone Your Fork Locally**

```sh
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/chirpy-starter.git
cd chirpy-starter
```

---

### 3. **Install Ruby, Bundler, and Jekyll**

If you haven’t already, install [Ruby](https://rubyinstaller.org/) and then run:

```sh
gem install bundler
bundle install
```

---

### 4. **Run the Site Locally**

```sh
bundle exec jekyll s
```
Visit [http://localhost:4000](http://localhost:4000) to preview.

---

### 5. **Configure Your Site**

Edit `_config.yml` to set your site title, description, author, etc.

---

### 6. **Deploy to GitHub Pages**

- Push your changes to your forked repo.
- In your repo settings on GitHub, enable GitHub Pages (usually from the `gh-pages` branch or `/docs` folder, as described in the [Chirpy docs](https://chirpy.cotes.page/posts/getting-started/)).

---

**Note:**  
Chirpy is not an “official” GitHub Pages theme, so you must build the site locally and push the generated files, or use GitHub Actions (recommended by Chirpy) for automatic builds.

---

**Docs:**  
See the [Chirpy Getting Started Guide](https://chirpy.cotes.page/posts/getting-started/) for full details.

Let me know if you want help with a specific step!

---

_**User**_

What are the rich features of chirpy compared to minimal mistakes?

---

_**Assistant**_

Here’s a comparison of **Chirpy** and **Minimal Mistakes** Jekyll themes, focusing on their rich features:

---

### **Chirpy**
- **Modern UI:** Clean, responsive, and blog-focused design.
- **Dark/Light Mode:** Built-in toggle for dark and light themes.
- **Search:** Built-in local search (no third-party service needed).
- **Table of Contents:** Automatic TOC for posts and pages.
- **Comments:** Supports multiple comment systems (Giscus, Disqus, Utterances, etc.).
- **Math Support:** Built-in support for math (KaTeX).
- **Code Highlighting:** Syntax highlighting with line numbers and copy button.
- **Post Navigation:** Next/previous post links.
- **Tags & Categories:** Tag and category pages auto-generated.
- **Social Sharing:** Built-in sharing buttons.
- **PWA Support:** Optional Progressive Web App features.
- **GitHub Actions:** Built-in workflow for automatic deployment.
- **SEO & Analytics:** SEO tags, Google Analytics, and more.
- **Customizable Sidebar:** Profile, recent posts, categories, etc.
- **Built-in Archive Pages:** Yearly/monthly archives.

---

### **Minimal Mistakes**
- **Highly Customizable:** Many layout options, skins, and configuration settings.
- **Dark Mode:** Multiple color skins, including dark.
- **Flexible Navigation:** Multiple navigation and sidebar options.
- **Search:** Supports local search (Lunr.js) and Algolia.
- **Comments:** Supports Disqus, Facebook, Staticman, etc.
- **Math Support:** MathJax and KaTeX support.
- **Code Highlighting:** Syntax highlighting.
- **Author Profiles:** Multiple authors, author pages.
- **Portfolio Layouts:** Project and portfolio layouts.
- **Collections:** Support for custom content types.
- **SEO & Analytics:** SEO tags, Google Analytics, and more.
- **Social Sharing:** Sharing buttons and social links.
- **Built-in Archive Pages:** Category, tag, and date archives.
- **Documentation:** Extensive documentation and examples.

---

### **Summary**
- **Chirpy** is more opinionated, modern, and blog-centric, with built-in features like dark mode toggle, local search, PWA, and GitHub Actions for deployment.
- **Minimal Mistakes** is more flexible and customizable, suitable for blogs, portfolios, and documentation, with many layout and content options.

**Choose Chirpy if:**  
You want a modern, feature-rich blog with minimal setup and built-in deployment.

**Choose Minimal Mistakes if:**  
You want maximum flexibility, custom layouts, or a portfolio/documentation site.

Let me know if you want a feature-by-feature table or have a specific use case!

---

_**User**_

I would like blogging eventually, but right now I'm mostly concerned with portfolio / resume stuff. Can chirpy do tile-like elements to show off projects, that link to pages dedicated to those projects?

---

_**Assistant**_

Chirpy is primarily designed for blogging and does not natively support portfolio “tiles” or grid-style project showcases out of the box like some portfolio-focused themes (e.g., Minimal Mistakes or So Simple). However, you **can** create a portfolio section in Chirpy with some manual setup:

### How to Create a Portfolio in Chirpy

1. **Create a Portfolio Page**  
   Add a new Markdown file (e.g., `portfolio.md`) in your root directory.

2. **Add Tile-like Elements**  
   Use Markdown or HTML to create a grid of project “tiles.” Each tile can link to a dedicated project page.

   Example:
   ````markdown
   # Portfolio

   <div class="portfolio-grid">
     <a href="/projects/project1/">
       <img src="/assets/img/project1-thumb.png" alt="Project 1" />
       <h3>Project 1</h3>
       <p>Short description of Project 1.</p>
     </a>
     <a href="/projects/project2/">
       <img src="/assets/img/project2-thumb.png" alt="Project 2" />
       <h3>Project 2</h3>
       <p>Short description of Project 2.</p>
     </a>
     <!-- Add more tiles as needed -->
   </div>
   ````

3. **Add Custom CSS**  
   To make the tiles display in a grid, add custom CSS to your assets (e.g., in `assets/css/style.scss`):

   ````css
   /* filepath: assets/css/style.scss */
   .portfolio-grid {
     display: flex;
     flex-wrap: wrap;
     gap: 2rem;
   }
   .portfolio-grid a {
     display: block;
     width: 250px;
     border: 1px solid #ddd;
     border-radius: 8px;
     padding: 1rem;
     text-align: center;
     text-decoration: none;
     background: var(--card-bg, #fff);
     color: inherit;
     transition: box-shadow 0.2s;
   }
   .portfolio-grid a:hover {
     box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.1);
   }
   .portfolio-grid img {
     max-width: 100%;
     height: auto;
     border-radius: 4px;
   }
   ````

4. **Create Dedicated Project Pages**  
   Add a folder for each project under `/projects/` with an `index.md` or `index.html` describing the project.

---

**Summary:**  
Chirpy can support a portfolio section, but you’ll need to create the grid and project pages manually. If you want built-in portfolio layouts, Minimal Mistakes or similar themes may be easier.

Let me know if you want a ready-to-use template or help with the CSS!

---

_**User**_

Sounds like Minimal Mistakes is going to be a lot easier

_**Assistant**_

Yes, **Minimal Mistakes** is much easier for portfolio and resume sites. It has built-in support for portfolio collections, project grids, and flexible layouts—no need for custom HTML or CSS. You can quickly create project pages and display them as tiles or cards with minimal setup.

_**User**_

I would definitely like that eventually. However, I want to troubleshoot an issue first. In trying to preview the site locally using Jekyll, I went through your earlier steps, but the browser preview is showing this image

---

_**Assistant**_

This “Index of /” directory listing means Jekyll is not processing your site as a Jekyll project. Common causes:

### 1. **Missing `_config.yml`**
Jekyll requires a `_config.yml` file in your project root. If it’s missing, Jekyll serves the folder as static files.

**Solution:**  
Create a `_config.yml` file in your project root. For Minimal Mistakes, a minimal example:

````yaml
title: "Matt Nolan Portfolio"
description: "My personal project portfolio"
theme: minimal-mistakes-jekyll
````

---

### 2. **Missing `index.html` or `index.md` Front Matter**
Your `index.md` file must have [YAML front matter](https://jekyllrb.com/docs/front-matter/) at the top, or Jekyll will ignore it.

**Solution:**  
Add front matter to `index.md`:

````markdown
---
layout: home
title: "Welcome"
---

# Welcome to My Portfolio

Here are a few things I’ve built.
````

---

### 3. **Wrong Directory**
Make sure you are running `bundle exec jekyll serve` from the project root (where your `_config.yml` is).

---

### 4. **Theme Not Installed**
Run `bundle install` after editing your `Gemfile` to ensure all gems are installed.

---

**Summary of Steps:**
1. Ensure you have a `_config.yml`.
2. Add front matter to `index.md`.
3. Run `bundle install`.
4. Run `bundle exec jekyll serve` from the project root.

Let me know if you need help with any of these steps!

---

_**User**_

Ok I added the front matter, and now it is showing the markdown rendering of index.md, but it does not appear to be using the theme. I'm trying to make a minimal example using jekyll-theme-cayman

---

_**Assistant**_

Great, you’re close! If your site is rendering the Markdown but **not** using the Cayman theme, it’s likely because `jekyll-theme-cayman` expects an `index.html` (not `index.md`) with the correct front matter, or your Markdown file isn’t being processed with the theme’s layout.

### How to Fix

#### 1. **Rename `index.md` to `index.html`**
Change your `index.md` to `index.html` and keep the front matter:

````html
---
layout: default
title: "Welcome"
---

# Welcome to My Portfolio

Here are a few things I’ve built.
````

#### 2. **Use the Correct Layout**
For Cayman, use `layout: default` in your front matter.

#### 3. **Check Your `_config.yml`**
You already have:
````yaml
theme: jekyll-theme-cayman
title: Matt Nolan's Portfolio
````

#### 4. **Rebuild the Site**
After making these changes, run:
```
bundle install
bundle exec jekyll serve
```

#### 5. **Visit [http://localhost:4000](http://localhost:4000)**
You should now see your content styled with the Cayman theme.

---

**Summary:**  
- Use `index.html` (not `index.md`)
- Use `layout: default`
- Rebuild and preview

Let me know if you see the Cayman theme now!

---

_**User**_

Ok, looks like it didn't care about the html vs md file type, but setting layout: default seems to have worked

---

_**Assistant**_

That’s correct! In Jekyll, both `.md` and `.html` files work as long as they have valid front matter and specify a layout. The key is the `layout: default` (or another layout provided by the theme) in the front matter—this tells Jekyll to wrap your content with the theme’s templates.

If you want to further customize the look or add navigation, just let me know!

---

_**Assistant**_

These are **deprecation warnings** from the Sass preprocessor, not errors. Your site will still build and work, but these warnings mean that the way Cayman (and possibly your custom CSS) imports stylesheets is outdated and will eventually stop working in future Sass versions.

### What’s Happening?
- The `@import` rule in Sass is deprecated in favor of `@use` and `@forward`.
- The Cayman theme and its dependencies haven’t updated to the new syntax yet.
- The `invert()` function is also deprecated in favor of `color.invert()`.

### What Should You Do?
- **You can safely ignore these warnings for now.** Your site will still work and look correct.
- If you want to suppress the warnings, you’d need to update the theme’s Sass files, but this is not necessary unless you’re customizing the theme heavily.
- When the Cayman theme updates to use `@use`/`@forward`, these warnings will go away.

### Optional: Add `wdm` Gem for Faster File Watching on Windows
Add this to your `Gemfile` for better performance on Windows:
````ruby
gem 'wdm', '>= 0.1.0' if Gem.win_platform?
````

Then run:
```
bundle install
```

---

**Summary:**  
- These are just warnings, not errors.
- Your site is working as expected.
- No action is required unless you want to future-proof or contribute fixes upstream.

Let me know if you have any other issues or want to switch to Minimal Mistakes!

---

_**User**_

Ok now I want to switch to minimal mistakes

---

_**Assistant**_

To switch your site to **Minimal Mistakes**, follow these steps:

---

### 1. **Update `_config.yml`**

Replace the `theme:` line with Minimal Mistakes:

````yaml
theme: minimal-mistakes-jekyll
title: Matt Nolan's Portfolio
````

---

### 2. **Update Your `Gemfile`**

Make sure you have only one theme active. Remove or comment out the Cayman theme line:

````ruby
gem "jekyll", "~> 4.4"
gem "minimal-mistakes-jekyll"
# gem "jekyll-theme-cayman"
````

Then run:
```
bundle install
```

---

### 3. **Update Your Home Page Front Matter**

Minimal Mistakes uses different layouts. For a simple homepage, use `layout: home`:

````markdown
---
layout: home
title: "Welcome"
---

# Welcome to My Portfolio

Here are a few things I’ve built.
````

---

### 4. **Serve Your Site**

Run:
```
bundle exec jekyll serve
```
and preview at [http://localhost:4000](http://localhost:4000).

---

**Tip:**  
Minimal Mistakes has many options! See [the docs](https://mmistakes.github.io/minimal-mistakes/docs/configuration/) for customizing navigation, adding a portfolio, and more.

Let me know if you want help setting up a portfolio grid or customizing the theme!

---

_**User**_

Ok that appears to work, I'll poke around the documentation for now and get back to you if I have any questions. Will I need to change how I specify the theme when I'm publishing via Github Pages?

---

_**Assistant**_

No, you do **not** need to change how you specify the theme when publishing with GitHub Pages if you’re using the `theme: minimal-mistakes-jekyll` line in your `_config.yml` and have the theme in your `Gemfile`.

**However:**  
- GitHub Pages only supports a limited set of themes natively. Minimal Mistakes is not one of them, so you must use **GitHub Actions** or build the site locally and push the generated `` folder, or use the [remote_theme](https://github.com/benbalter/jekyll-remote-theme) method.

**Recommended for Minimal Mistakes:**  
- Use the [remote_theme](https://github.com/benbalter/jekyll-remote-theme) approach for easiest GitHub Pages integration:

````yaml
remote_theme: "mmistakes/minimal-mistakes"
````

- Remove or comment out the `theme:` line if you use `remote_theme:`.
- You do **not** need the theme in your `Gemfile` if you use `remote_theme:` and are building on GitHub Pages.

**Summary:**  
- For local builds: `theme: minimal-mistakes-jekyll` in `_config.yml` and gem in `Gemfile`.
- For GitHub Pages: Prefer `remote_theme: mmistakes/minimal-mistakes` in `_config.yml`.

Let me know if you want a step-by-step for deploying with Minimal Mistakes on GitHub Pages!

---

_**User**_

Can I use the remote_theme option while I'm building it locally?

---

_**Assistant**_

Yes, you can use the `remote_theme` option while building locally!  
However, you need to install the `jekyll-remote-theme` plugin for local builds to work.

**Steps:**

1. **In your `_config.yml`:**
   ````yaml
   remote_theme: "mmistakes/minimal-mistakes"
   ````

2. **In your `Gemfile`:**
   ````ruby
   gem "jekyll-remote-theme"
   ````

3. **In your `_config.yml`, add the plugin:**
   ````yaml
   plugins:
     - jekyll-remote-theme
   ````

4. **Install dependencies:**
   ```
   bundle install
   ```

5. **Serve your site:**
   ```
   bundle exec jekyll serve
   ```

---

This setup lets you preview your site locally with the remote theme, just like it will appear on GitHub Pages.

---

_**User**_

@terminal /explain #terminalSelection

---

_**Assistant**_

You are getting a NoMethodError because the jekyll-remote-theme plugin you are using is not compatible with your version of Jekyll or Ruby. The method configure_sass no longer exists or has changed in recent versions.

**How to fix:**

1. **Update jekyll-remote-theme**  
   Open your terminal and run:
   cmd
   gem update jekyll-remote-theme

2. **Update your Gemfile**  
   Make sure your Gemfile has the latest versions:
   ruby
   gem "jekyll", "~> 4.3"
   gem "jekyll-remote-theme", "~> 0.4.3"

   Then run:
   cmd
   bundle install

3. **If the issue persists:**  
   - Downgrade Ruby to 3.3.x (Jekyll may not fully support Ruby 3.4 yet).
   - Or, downgrade jekyll-remote-theme to a version compatible with your Jekyll and Ruby.

**Summary:**  
This is a version mismatch. Update your gems, and if needed, use an older Ruby version.

---

_**User**_

Can you re-format this advice? The code suggestions are appearing in plain text

---

_**Assistant**_

Certainly! Here’s the advice reformatted with proper code blocks:

---

You are getting a `NoMethodError` because the `jekyll-remote-theme` plugin you are using is not compatible with your version of Jekyll or Ruby. The method `configure_sass` no longer exists or has changed in recent versions.

### How to Fix

1. **Update `jekyll-remote-theme`**

   In your terminal, run:
   ````sh
   gem update jekyll-remote-theme
   ````

2. **Update your Gemfile**

   Make sure your `Gemfile` has the latest versions:
   ````ruby
   gem "jekyll", "~> 4.3"
   gem "jekyll-remote-theme", "~> 0.4.3"
   ````

   Then run:
   ````sh
   bundle install
   ````

3. **If the issue persists:**
   - Downgrade Ruby to 3.3.x (Jekyll may not fully support Ruby 3.4 yet).
   - Or, downgrade `jekyll-remote-theme` to a version compatible with your Jekyll and Ruby.

---

**Summary:**  
This is a version mismatch. Update your gems, and if needed, use an older Ruby version.

---

---

_**Assistant**_

You are now getting this error:

```
Liquid Exception: Liquid syntax error (line 10): Unknown tag 'include_cached' in .../_layouts/default.html
...
Liquid::SyntaxError: Unknown tag 'include_cached'
```

This means the **Minimal Mistakes theme requires the `jekyll-include-cache` plugin**, which is missing from your setup.

---

## How to Fix

1. **Add the plugin to your Gemfile:**
   ````ruby
   # filepath: Gemfile
   gem "jekyll-include-cache"
   ````

2. **Add the plugin to your `_config.yml`:**
   ````yaml
   # filepath: _config.yml
   plugins:
     - jekyll-remote-theme
     - jekyll-include-cache
   ````

3. **Install the new gem:**
   ```
   bundle install
   ```

4. **Serve your site again:**
   ```
   bundle exec jekyll serve
   ```

---

This should resolve the `include_cached` error and allow Minimal Mistakes to work locally. Let me know if you hit any other issues!

---
